# Provider transparency — who tells you when they change how they serve a model

<!-- GENERATED by scripts/make_provider_transparency.py from findings/provider_transparency.json + findings/provider_transparency_sources.json. Do not edit by hand. -->

The rest of this project asks whether *researchers* pin their provider routing. This file asks the mirror-image question: if a researcher wanted to do the right thing by reading the vendor's own disclosures, **could they?** When a provider changes the precision, the context cap, the inference engine, or the weights behind a model ID, does it say so anywhere a caller could find, in a dated form?

Mostly not. And the way it fails is specific enough to be useful.

## Three different things "transparent" can mean

Providers that look comparable on a marketing page turn out to occupy different axes entirely, and a provider strong on one is routinely silent on another:

| Axis | The question it answers |
| --- | --- |
| **A** — serves-now | per-model precision, context, engine |
| **B** — when-it-changed | a dated changelog of serving changes |
| **C** — what-happens-next | deprecation policy with a stated notice period |

**10 providers were verified against the live page; 12 are unchecked leads.** Every quotation below was located verbatim in a snapshot of the cited page taken by `scripts/fetch_provider_transparency.py`. The leads were surfaced by one pass of search agents and confirmed by nobody; they carry no quotation and must not be cited as evidence until someone verifies them. This is a much thinner evidence base than [`prior-work.md`](prior-work.md) and should be read as a map of where to look, not a finished audit.

---

## Verified

### Cerebras

*[https://inference-docs.cerebras.ai/models/overview](https://inference-docs.cerebras.ai/models/overview) · discloses on: **A** serves-now, **C** what-happens-next*

> We are committed to serving the original models for all existing endpoints, without modification.
>
> All of our public models are unpruned.

The only forward-looking commitment found anywhere in the sweep. Its precision claim is unusually specific and therefore falsifiable: weight-only quantization applied at storage, with activations, attention and the KV cache left unquantized. Cerebras is the provider a reviewer could most easily hold to its word.

**Gap:** No quantified deprecation notice period. The deprecations page is a dated log, not a policy.

### Azure AI Foundry

*[https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/concepts/model-retirements](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/concepts/model-retirements) · discloses on: **C** what-happens-next*

> Production-ready. Weights and APIs are fixed. Runtime patches for security vulnerabilities don't affect outputs.
>
> Experimental. Weights, runtime, and API schema might change. Not guaranteed to become GA.
>
> Auto-upgrades are scheduled on a rolling, region-by-region basis. The upgrade schedule is published in advance

The strongest written weight-fixity guarantee in the sweep, and the only one stated as a lifecycle contract rather than a convention. It also ships the corresponding knob: `versionUpgradeOption: NoAutoUpgrade` refuses the silent forward-roll entirely, which is the hard pin this project recommends rather than a floor. Lifecycle is queryable programmatically via `lifecycleStatus` and `deprecation.inference`, and Azure documents a concrete past mutation: gpt-4o 2024-05-13 and 2024-08-06 auto-upgraded to gpt-5.1 on 2026-03-31 across eight named regions.

**Gap:** Never states serving precision. Preview deployments are force-upgraded with no opt-out.

### Cloudflare Workers AI

*[https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers-ai/models/llama-3.3-70b-instruct-fp8-fast/](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers-ai/models/llama-3.3-70b-instruct-fp8-fast/) · discloses on: **A** serves-now, **B** when-it-changed*

> Llama 3.3 70B quantized to fp8 precision, optimized to be faster.

Precision is in the model ID and stated in the docs, alongside a 24,000-token context window. The catalog is generated from JSON in the public cloudflare/cloudflare-docs repository, so a change to a model's declared precision or context is a dated git commit anyone can diff - the only provider in the sweep whose serving spec has a reconstructible history rather than just a current value.

**Gap:** No immutable pinning: deprecated slugs are aliased forward to a successor rather than frozen.

### Together AI

*[https://docs.together.ai/docs/changelog](https://docs.together.ai/docs/changelog) · discloses on: **A** serves-now, **B** when-it-changed, **C** what-happens-next*

> 552.8B parameters, 524,288 context length, NVFP4 quantization

The best dated changelog: entries name precision and context at launch, and deprecations carry stated notice periods.

**Gap:** Precision is announced inconsistently - of five recent serverless launches checked, only one stated quantization. Their policy also permits an 'upgrade' to silently redirect an existing model ID after a three-day notice, which is the single most dangerous clause found in any provider's documentation for research use.

### xAI

*[https://docs.x.ai/docs/models](https://docs.x.ai/docs/models) · discloses on: **C** what-happens-next*

> This will not be updated and is for workflows that demand consistency.
>
> For most users, the aliased <modelname> or <modelname>-latest are recommended, as you would receive the latest features automatically.

States the pin guarantee explicitly and, unusually, names the tradeoff: the floating alias is recommended for most users, the dated ID for anyone who needs consistency. That is exactly the distinction research code has to make.

**Gap:** No changelog or deprecation policy found. No precision disclosure.

### Anthropic

*[https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/model-deprecations](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/model-deprecations) · discloses on: **C** what-happens-next*

> Anthropic notifies customers with active deployments for models with upcoming retirements, providing at least 60 days' notice before model retirement for publicly released models.

A complete dated deprecation history back to 2024 with recommended replacements, plus an explicit Active/Legacy/Deprecated/Retired taxonomy and a commitment to long-term preservation of retired model weights. Best-in-class on lifecycle.

**Gap:** No written guarantee that weights behind a dated ID are fixed - it is a convention, not a promise. Never states serving precision.

### OpenAI

*[https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/deprecations](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/deprecations) · discloses on: **C** what-happens-next*

> Generally available models: At least 6 months

The longest stated notice period of any provider in the sweep, with dated retirement tables running back to 2022.

**Gap:** The deprecations page carries no statement that the weights behind a dated snapshot ID will not change. Never states serving precision.

### DeepSeek

*[https://api-docs.deepseek.com/quick_start/pricing](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/quick_start/pricing) · discloses on: **B** when-it-changed*

*The finding is an absence: the page lists deepseek-v4-flash and deepseek-v4-pro as the callable model names, and no dated identifier appears among them.*

The structurally weakest provider verified. Dated tags such as DeepSeek-R1-0528 are open-weight release labels, not API model parameters - there is no way to pin a DeepSeek API call to a fixed snapshot at all. To their credit the changelog does date the in-place checkpoint swaps, which is more than most providers offer; there is simply no pinned alternative to switch to.

**Gap:** No pinnable ID exists. No precision disclosure. No general deprecation policy.

### Groq

*[https://api.github.com/repos/groq/groq-changelog](https://api.github.com/repos/groq/groq-changelog) · discloses on: **B** when-it-changed*

*Verified by API metadata rather than page text: the repository groq/groq-changelog exists and is public, described as 'Groq Public Changelog'.*

The changelog is a public git repository with an Atom feed on its commits, which makes it the most machine-followable changelog in the sweep - a diff, not a page.

**Gap:** Publishes no precision anywhere, and reports quantization 'unknown' to OpenRouter. Excellent on axis B, absent on axis A.

### Chutes

*[https://llm.chutes.ai/v1/models](https://llm.chutes.ai/v1/models) · discloses on: **A** serves-now*

*Verified against the live JSON API rather than page text: each model object carries quantization, context_length, max_output_length and owned_by.*

The most complete machine-readable disclosure of a current serving config found anywhere. Chutes is the only provider in the sweep that publishes the inference engine per model (owned_by returns sglang or vllm) alongside precision and both token limits. Per-deployment source is inspectable with a locked model revision, and a watchdog spot-checks served weight files by hashing them at random offsets.

**Gap:** No changelog and no deprecation policy - it tells you the current config precisely and its history not at all.

---

## Unchecked leads

Reported by a search agent, not verified. No quotations, by construction.

- **Fireworks AI** — [docs](https://docs.fireworks.ai/updates/changelog) · **B** when-it-changed, **C** what-happens-next  
  Reported: dated changelog with serverless deprecation entries and an 'at least 2 weeks notice' policy. Reported to fail on precision for a specific reason worth re-checking - its prominent quantization documentation covers customers quantizing their own dedicated deployments, not what runs on the shared serverless endpoint.
- **Parasail** — [docs](https://docs.parasail.io/parasail-docs/products/overview-1/fp8-quantization) · —  
  Reported: same customer-deployment trap as Fireworks. Reported that pricing distinguishes FP8 from FP16 tiers without naming which serverless models are which - i.e. the information exists internally and is not published.
- **SiliconFlow** — [docs](https://docs.siliconflow.com/en/release-notes/overview) · **B** when-it-changed  
  Reported: dates its in-place weight swaps, including a DeepSeek-R1 checkpoint change under an unchanged model ID. If confirmed this would be one of the few providers that logs the mutation rather than only the launch and the death - worth verifying first of the unchecked leads.
- **Baseten** — [docs](https://www.baseten.co/blog/how-we-built-the-worlds-fastest-api-for-glm-52/) · **B** when-it-changed  
  Reported: a blog post describing an FP8-to-NVFP4 change on a production shared endpoint. Reported to be the only dated precision-CHANGE announcement in the entire sweep, which makes it the highest-value lead to confirm.
- **Nebius** — [docs](https://docs.tokenfactory.nebius.com/api-reference/examples/list-of-models) · **A** serves-now  
  Reported: the models API returns a per-model quantization field. Reported to have no changelog covering model serving.
- **Featherless** — [docs](https://featherless.ai/docs/models-model-compatibility) · **A** serves-now  
  Reported: a platform-wide rule that models are served at FP8, with models under 5B at FP16.
- **DigitalOcean** — [docs](https://docs.digitalocean.com/products/inference/details/model-support-policy/) · **C** what-happens-next  
  Reported: an explicit 14-day / 7-day / 0-day retirement ladder with a dated end-of-support table.
- **SambaNova** — [docs](https://docs.sambanova.ai/docs/en/models/deprecations) · **C** what-happens-next  
  Reported: a two-to-three week notice policy with a dated table. Also reported to have given two contradictory official answers about DeepSeek serving precision in its own forum - if confirmed, a useful example of why vendor testimony needs ranking.
- **Amazon Bedrock** — [docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-lifecycle.html) · **C** what-happens-next  
  Reported: a 12-month minimum on-service commitment, a dated retirement table, no floating alias at the base-model level, and a modelLifecycle field in the API.
- **Friendli** — [docs](https://friendli.ai/docs/changelog) · **B** when-it-changed  
  Reported: dated changelog entries naming specific model IDs as they are removed.
- **Venice AI** — [docs](https://featurebase.venice.ai/changelog) · **B** when-it-changed  
  Reported: dated changelog entries naming serving precision, and retirements that reroute an old ID to a different model via alias.
- **Phala Network** — [docs](https://docs.redpill.ai/confidential-ai/attestation-report) · —  
  Reported with an important correction to the marketing framing: the TEE attestation proves the hardware and the serving-code build (repo commit, image digest), but the attestation report was reported to carry no field for model hash, weights or quantization. Attestation of the runtime is not attestation of the model. Worth verifying because the distinction matters for anyone treating TEE providers as reproducible.

---

## The one number that is ours

Everything above is vendor testimony — the weakest evidence class this project recognises. So we measured the thing vendors cannot spin: whether they declare a quantization to OpenRouter at all.

Across **42 serving endpoints** for 6 widely-served open-weight models, **31% (13) declare no quantization** — the field comes back `unknown`.

| Declared quantization | Endpoints |
| --- | --- |
| *(undeclared)* | 13 |
| `fp8` | 12 |
| `fp4` | 9 |
| `bf16` | 6 |
| `fp16` | 2 |

**7 providers declared nothing across the entire sample:** Alibaba, Amazon Bedrock, DigitalOcean, Google, Groq, Mara, Phala.

17 declared a real precision somewhere: AkashML, AtlasCloud, BaseTen, Cerebras, Cloudflare, DeepInfra, DekaLLM, Mancer 2, Nebius, Novita, Parasail, SambaNova, SiliconFlow, Together, Venice, WandB, Z.AI.

Sample: `openai/gpt-oss-120b`, `qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b`, `deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324`, `meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct`, `moonshotai/kimi-k2`, `z-ai/glm-4.6`.

---

## What the sweep actually found

**Nobody logs the mutation.** Providers announce launches and they announce retirements. Almost none publish a dated record of a serving-config change to an endpoint that continues to exist under the same name — which is precisely the event that silently invalidates a research result, because nothing in the caller's code changes. Cloudflare's git-tracked catalog is the one place where such a change is reconstructible, and it is an accident of publishing the catalog as source rather than a deliberate disclosure.

**The two strengths are held by disjoint sets of providers.** The hyperscalers and first-party labs are excellent on lifecycle — dated snapshot IDs, quantified notice periods, retirement tables — and *never* state serving precision. The GPU clouds are the reverse: several publish precision per model, and few can tell you when it last changed. No provider verified here does both well.

**Vendor testimony contradicts itself often enough to need ranking.** Two of the leads below are cases where a provider's marketing claim and its own engineers' statements disagree about serving precision. This is the concrete reason this project ranks our measurement above independent prior work above vendor assertion.

## What this means for the guidance

**No provider changelog is a substitute for recording the endpoint yourself.** Even the best-documented provider gives a caller a point-in-time spec, not a diffable history, and the majority give nothing at all. The practice the skill should recommend is therefore unchanged by anything in this file: pin the provider, and snapshot `/api/v1/models/{slug}/endpoints` at run time alongside the results, so the serving config is recorded in *your* artifact rather than trusted to the vendor's.

Two findings do sharpen the guidance:

- **A written weight-fixity guarantee exists and is worth asking for.** Azure states it as a lifecycle contract and ships `versionUpgradeOption: NoAutoUpgrade` to enforce it. That is a hard pin, not a floor, and it is the shape other vendors should be held to.
- **A pinnable ID is not universal.** DeepSeek's API exposes only floating names, so for that provider there is no correct configuration to recommend — only measurement after the fact.
